schools.summary: The exhortation continuing the theme of submission (2:13ff.): 'likewise, ye wives, be in subjection (submit yourselves) to your own husbands' — Christian wives to submit to their husbands in the ordered relation of the household ('likewise' linking it to the general duty of submission); 'that, if any obey not the word (are unbelievers, do not accept the gospel), they also may without the word (without preaching or argument) be won (gained to the faith) by the conversation (anastrophe — manner of life, conduct) of the wives' — the special case of a wife with an unbelieving husband, whose holy, submissive behaviour may win him to Christ more powerfully than words, a godly life preaching where speech cannot (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: ye wives, be in subjection to your own husbands
if any obey not the word, they also may without the word be won by the conversation of the wives
the call to wives to win unbelieving husbands by their conduct
the silent witness of a holy life

schools.summary: How the husband is won: 'while they behold (observe, watch) your chaste (pure) conversation (anastrophe — conduct, manner of life) coupled with fear (reverence)' — the unbelieving husband, watching his wife's pure and reverent behaviour day by day, moved by what he sees; 'chaste conversation' a pure, modest, holy manner of life (blameless conduct that adorns the gospel, the wife's purity the silent argument); 'with fear' being either reverence toward God or respectful regard toward the husband, a holy fear that keeps the conduct pure and modest — the chaste life and the reverent spirit together winning the husband (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: While they behold your chaste conversation coupled with fear
the chaste conduct joined with fear that wins the husband
a pure, modest, holy manner of life
reverence keeping the conduct pure

schools.summary: The counsel on adornment: 'whose adorning (kosmos — ornamentation) let it not be that outward adorning of plaiting (braiding, elaborate arrangement) the hair, and of wearing of gold (gold jewelry), or of putting on of apparel (fine clothing)' — the wife's true adornment not to consist in elaborate hairstyles, gold ornaments, and fine clothes; not an absolute prohibition of all clothing or ornament (which would be absurd) but a warning against making outward show the chief adornment (the emphasis on 'let it not be' the principal thing, excessive vain external display checked); the warning against vanity and worldliness in dress, which betray a wrong estimate of what is precious, true beauty being inward (v.4) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: whose adorning let it not be that outward adorning of plaiting the hair, and of wearing of gold, or of putting on of apparel
the warning against mere outward adornment
outward show not the chief adornment
vanity in dress checked

schools.summary: The contrast with outward show: 'but let it be the hidden (inner, concealed) man of the heart (the inward person, the character of the soul), in that which is not corruptible (incorruptible, enduring), even the ornament of a meek (gentle) and quiet (tranquil, peaceable) spirit, which is in the sight of God of great price (very costly, precious)' — the true adornment being the inward self (the hidden character, not the outward appearance), an incorruptible ornament unlike fading external finery; gentleness and tranquility of soul the true and lasting beauty, which God esteems as of great worth (what the world counts plain, God counts precious, his estimate not the world's defining true beauty) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: let it be the hidden man of the heart... even the ornament of a meek and quiet spirit
which is in the sight of God of great price
the true adornment: the meek and quiet spirit, precious to God
incorruptible inward beauty

schools.summary: The appeal to precedent: 'for after this manner in the old time the holy women (the godly women of the Old Testament) also, who trusted (hoped) in God, adorned themselves, being in subjection unto their own husbands' — the saintly women of old adorning themselves in this inward way, with the meek spirit not outward finery; their adornment springing from faith (their hope in God, not in outward show, the inward beauty the fruit of their trust); their true ornament including a submissive, reverent spirit toward their husbands (submission part of their inward beauty), the example setting the pattern for Christian wives (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: the holy women also, who trusted in God, adorned themselves, being in subjection unto their own husbands
the holy women of old adorned with submission and trust
inward beauty the fruit of faith
the pattern for Christian wives

schools.summary: The chief example: 'even as Sara obeyed (hypekouen — submitted to) Abraham, calling him lord (a title of respect, Genesis 18:12): whose daughters ye are, as long as ye do well, and are not afraid with any amazement (any terror, not driven by fear)' — Sarah the supreme example, who obeyed her husband and called him 'lord,' showing respectful submission; Christian wives becoming her true daughters (her spiritual offspring) by imitating her faith and submission (as believers are sons of Abraham by faith, so godly wives are daughters of Sarah); the condition being doing good and not being overcome by fear (acting from faith not terror, living uprightly without being frightened from their duty) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: even as Sara obeyed Abraham, calling him lord: whose daughters ye are
as long as ye do well, and are not afraid with any amazement
Sarah's example, whose daughters the faithful wives are
becoming Sarah's daughters by faith and submission

schools.summary: The reciprocal duty: 'likewise, ye husbands, dwell with them according to knowledge (with understanding, considerately, wisely), giving honour (respect, esteem) unto the wife, as unto the weaker (more delicate, physically frailer) vessel, and as being heirs together of the grace of life (joint heirs of eternal life); that your prayers be not hindered (interrupted, obstructed)' — the husband to live with his wife thoughtfully (the duty mutual, not submission only on the wife's part), honouring her, her being the 'weaker vessel' a reason for honour and tender care not contempt (weakness calling for protection and respect); husband and wife equal before God as joint heirs of grace; and the husband to so live that marital discord or failure in duty not hinder his prayers (harmony necessary for unhindered fellowship with God) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: ye husbands, dwell with them according to knowledge, giving honour unto the wife, as unto the weaker vessel
as being heirs together of the grace of life; that your prayers be not hindered
the husband's duty to dwell with the wife in honour as a joint heir
weakness a call for honour, discord hindering prayer

schools.summary: The summary exhortation to all believers: 'finally, be ye all of one mind (homophrones — harmonious, of one accord, like-minded), having compassion (sympatheis — sympathizing, feeling with) one of another, love as brethren (philadelphoi — loving as a family), be pitiful (eusplanchnoi — tenderhearted, compassionate), be courteous (philophrones/tapeinophrones — friendly, kind, humble-minded)' — all believers (not wives and husbands only) to be united in mind and purpose (harmony in the church), to share one another's joys and sorrows, to love one another with the affection of brothers, and to be tenderhearted and graciously kind — the cluster of social graces that should mark the Christian community (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: be ye all of one mind, having compassion one of another, love as brethren, be pitiful, be courteous
the call to unity, compassion, brotherly love, pity, and courtesy
the graces of the Christian community
harmony, sympathy, and kindness

schools.summary: The command: 'not rendering (paying back, returning) evil for evil, or railing (loidorian — abuse, reviling) for railing: but contrariwise (on the contrary) blessing; knowing that ye are thereunto called (to this very thing), that ye should inherit a blessing' — the believer not to retaliate (not to repay injury with injury or insult with insult, the renunciation of revenge) but instead to bless those who wrong him (returning good for evil, blessing for cursing, cf. Matthew 5:44; Romans 12:17); believers called to bless precisely so that they may inherit a blessing, God's calling and the promised inheritance both bound up with the blessing spirit (those who bless are blessed) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: not rendering evil for evil, or railing for railing: but contrariwise blessing
knowing that ye are thereunto called, that ye should inherit a blessing
the call to return blessing for evil
those who bless inherit a blessing

schools.summary: The quotation (Psalm 34:12-16) grounding the call to bless: 'for he that will love life, and see good days (he who desires to enjoy life and see good days), let him refrain (restrain, keep) his tongue from evil, and his lips that they speak no guile (deceit, treachery)' — the one who would love life must control his speech, keeping it free from evil and deceit; the first requirement of the good life being to guard the tongue (keep it from evil words, slander, and reviling, right speech foundational to the blessed life), the lips free not only of open evil but of deceit (sincerity and truthfulness marking the speech of the one who would see good days) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: he that will love life, and see good days, let him refrain his tongue from evil, and his lips that they speak no guile
the counsel from the Psalm: guard the tongue to love life
right speech foundational to the good life
lips free of evil and deceit

schools.summary: The Psalm's further requirements (Psalm 34): 'let him eschew (turn away from, shun) evil, and do good; let him seek peace, and ensue it (pursue it, follow after it)' — beyond guarded speech, the good life requiring turning from evil, doing good, and actively pursuing peace; the twofold duty being not merely avoiding evil but actively doing good (the blessed life both departure from evil and pursuit of good); peace not merely to be desired but actively sought and pursued, as one chases what flees, the believer to be a diligent peacemaker (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Let him eschew evil, and do good; let him seek peace, and ensue it
the counsel to turn from evil, do good, and pursue peace
not merely avoiding evil but doing good
peace diligently sought

schools.summary: The Psalm's ground (Psalm 34): 'for the eyes of the Lord are over (upon) the righteous, and his ears are open unto their prayers: but the face of the Lord is against (set against, opposed to) them that do evil' — God watching over the righteous with favour and care (his providential eye on them for good), hearing and answering their prayers (the blessed life of the godly enjoying access to God and the assurance of being heard); but his countenance turned against evildoers in displeasure and judgment, the same God who favours the righteous opposing the wicked — a double motive both to do good and to fear doing evil (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the Lord's eyes watch over the righteous and his ears are open to their prayers, but his face is set against evildoers; God's favourable regard rests on the godly who seek peace, while his judicial opposition falls on those who do evil — the assurance of the one and the warning of the other.

themes: the eyes of the Lord are over the righteous, and his ears are open unto their prayers
the face of the Lord is against them that do evil
the Lord's care for the righteous and his opposition to evildoers
favour to the godly, judgment on the wicked

schools.summary: The encouraging rhetorical question: 'and who is he that will harm (injure, do real evil to) you, if ye be followers (zelotai — zealous imitators, eager pursuers) of that which is good?' — as a general rule those devoted to doing good being protected (few wishing to harm, and none able truly to harm, the one zealous for good); 'followers of that which is good' meaning those passionately devoted to good, their goodness itself a protection (disarming enemies and winning favour); the general rule that goodness protects, though (as v.14 acknowledges) the righteous may still suffer, the deeper meaning being that no real, ultimate harm can come to the one zealous for good, for God watches over him (v.12) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: who is he that will harm you, if ye be followers of that which is good
the question: who will harm those zealous for good
goodness itself a protection
no real harm to the one God watches

schools.summary: The beatitude echoing Christ's words (Matthew 5:10): 'but and if ye suffer for righteousness' sake (on account of doing right), happy (blessed) are ye' — even when the godly do suffer for doing right, they are blessed (suffering for righteousness no calamity but a blessedness); 'and be not afraid of their terror (do not fear what they fear, or be frightened by their threats), neither be troubled (agitated, disturbed in heart)' — the citation of Isaiah 8:12-13, the believer not to be terrified by the threats or dread that persecutors inspire (the fear of man cast off), the heart to remain untroubled and at peace even amid persecution (the antidote following, v.15, sanctifying the Lord in the heart) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: if ye suffer for righteousness' sake, happy are ye
be not afraid of their terror, neither be troubled
suffering for righteousness is blessedness, free from fear
the fear of man cast off

schools.summary: The antidote to fear (the best texts read 'sanctify Christ as Lord'): 'but sanctify the Lord God (set apart, hallow, reverence the Lord, or Christ as Lord) in your hearts: and be ready always to give an answer (apologian — a defense, reasoned account) to every man that asketh you a reason (logon — an account) of the hope that is in you with meekness (gentleness, not arrogance or contention) and fear (reverence, humility, godly seriousness)' — to sanctify the Lord in the heart being to give him the supreme place (to reverence and trust him above all, this inward enthronement casting out the fear of man); the believer always to be prepared to explain and defend the Christian hope when asked (an intelligent, ready account of one's faith), the defense made humbly and reverently not harshly (the spirit of the answer mattering as much as its substance) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: sanctify the Lord God in your hearts
be ready always to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope that is in you with meekness and fear
the call to sanctify the Lord and be ready to give a reason for hope
the humble, reverent defense of the faith

schools.summary: The necessary accompaniment of the defense: 'having a good conscience (a clear conscience, integrity of life); that, whereas they speak evil of you, as of evildoers (slander you as wrongdoers), they may be ashamed (put to shame) that falsely accuse your good conversation (anastrophe — conduct) in Christ' — the believer's defense of his hope backed by a blameless life (a good conscience giving boldness and silencing slander); the persecutors falsely accusing the Christians of being evildoers, the good conscience and good conduct being the answer to such slander, so that the believers' good Christian conduct puts the false accusers to shame (the holy life refuting the slander and shaming those who uttered it) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Having a good conscience; that, whereas they speak evil of you, as of evildoers
they may be ashamed that falsely accuse your good conversation in Christ
the good conscience that shames false accusers
the holy life refuting slander

schools.summary: The comparison: 'for it is better, if the will of God be so (if God so wills it), that ye suffer for well doing (on account of doing good), than for evil doing (on account of doing wrong)' — if one must suffer, far better to suffer for doing right than for doing wrong (the cause of the suffering making all the difference); suffering for righteousness coming only 'if the will of God be so' (under God's permission and appointment, the believer's sufferings within God's will, which both limits and sanctifies them); to suffer for well-doing honourable and blessed (v.14), bringing no shame, while to suffer for evil-doing is deserved and shameful — the believer to make sure that if he suffers it is for the right cause (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: it is better, if the will of God be so, that ye suffer for well doing, than for evil doing
it is better to suffer for well doing than for evil doing
the cause of suffering making the difference
suffering within God's will

schools.summary: The supreme example and ground: 'for Christ also hath once (hapax — once for all) suffered for sins (peri hamartion — on account of sins, as a sin-offering), the just (the righteous one) for the unjust (the unrighteous), that he might bring us to God (lead us, give us access to God), being put to death in the flesh (in his human body, as to his bodily life), but quickened (made alive) by the Spirit (en pneumati — in spirit)' — Christ suffering once for all for sins, the righteous in the place of the unrighteous (the substitutionary atonement, the model and motive for patient suffering), aiming to reconcile us to God and bring the estranged into his presence; put to death in the flesh but made alive in the spirit (interpreters differing whether 'the Spirit' is the Holy Spirit, Christ's own divine nature, or his human spirit, but the contrast being between death in the body and life in the spirit) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: Christ also hath once suffered for sins, the just for the unjust, that he might bring us to God
being put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit
Christ's once-for-all suffering, the just for the unjust
the atonement bringing us to God

schools.summary: Barnes (with Clarke, Wesley, Gill) holds that Christ, by his Spirit (v.18), preached through Noah to the antediluvians while they were yet alive — the 'spirits in prison' being those people, now (when Peter wrote) confined in the prison of hades, but who heard the message when living, during the 120 years the ark was preparing (Genesis 6:3; Hebrews 11:7; 2 Peter 2:5, Noah a 'preacher of righteousness'). The 'going' is not a literal descent but a preaching by his Spirit through his servant Noah (as God is said to 'come and preach,' Ephesians 2:17; 'Qui facit per alium, facit per se'). Barnes argues this fits the context (an example of patient witness under opposition, encouraging persecuted believers) and that no doctrine of a second chance after death can be built on so difficult a passage; Augustine held 'Christ was in Noah when he preached'; Beza, Pearson, Hammond, and Leighton agree. The word 'preached' (ekeryxen, proclaimed as a herald) is general, not necessarily the gospel (euangelizo). All reject the Roman Catholic use of the text for purgatory or freeing the patriarchs from limbo.

Ellicott and Robertson hold the passage refers to what Christ did, in his disembodied spirit, between his death and resurrection: 'in spirit' (en pneumati, corresponding to 'quickened in spirit,' v.18) he went to the actual disembodied spirits in prison and made proclamation to them. Ellicott calls this the view of 'practically all the Fathers, and of Calvin, Luther (finally), Bellarmine, Bengel, and most modern scholars,' the most natural construction of 'in which also... he went,' giving point to 'quickened in spirit'; the 'prison' (phylake) being used 38 times in the NT for a prison, the spirits there disembodied (cf. 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 6; Revelation 20:7). Robertson notes Bigg's view that Christ preached to those who once heard Noah (now in prison), but cautions it is 'a slim hope' for those who neglect Christ to gamble on a second chance resting on 'very precarious exegesis of a most difficult passage'; Luther admits 'he does not know what Peter means.'

Calvin holds the words must be understood of the dead (living men are never called 'spirits'), rejecting the notion of Christ's soul descending into hell ('he went by the Spirit,' not in soul) and the apostolic-ministry allegory. He takes it that the vivifying power of Christ's Spirit was manifested to godly/dead spirits, that they were endued with its life — the 'prison' (phylake) rather a watchtower in which the holy fathers watched in hope of the promised salvation, or the prison of the Law (Galatians 3:23) under which they were kept until the spirit of liberty was given. He also reports favourably the view that Christ by his Spirit employed Noah as a preacher of righteousness to those whose spirits are now in prison (reserved for judgment), repeated in 1 Peter 4:6. Calvin firmly rejects that unbelievers were freed after death, since salvation is only by faith.

debates.question: When and to whom did Christ 'preach to the spirits in prison'?

sides.position: Through Noah's ministry, by his Spirit, to the antediluvians while they were alive (now 'spirits in prison'); no literal descent
In his own disembodied spirit, between death and resurrection, to the actual dead spirits in prison (the descent view)
The Roman Catholic reading: a descent to purgatory / to free the patriarchs from limbo (named and rejected by the Protestant voices)
holders: Roman Catholic interpreters (rejected by Gill, Clarke, Barnes)

themes: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison
Christ preaching to the spirits in prison
the famously contested descent/preaching crux
through Noah to the living, or in spirit to the dead

schools.summary: The specification of the spirits (v.19): 'which sometime (formerly, once) were disobedient (rebellious, unbelieving), when once the longsuffering of God waited in the days of Noah, while the ark was a preparing, wherein few, that is, eight souls were saved by water' — the spirits in prison identified as the disobedient generation of Noah's day, on whom God's patience waited; 'the longsuffering of God waited' in patient forbearance 120 years (Genesis 6:3) while the ark was being built, giving the wicked generation time to repent through Noah's preaching; only eight persons (Noah and his family, Genesis 7:7) saved, and saved 'by water' — borne up by the very waters that destroyed the rest (the encouragement that, though the faithful are few, God preserves them) — Barnes, Robertson, Clarke, Calvin, Henry.

themes: which sometime were disobedient, when once the longsuffering of God waited in the days of Noah
few, that is, eight souls were saved by water
the disobedient of Noah's day and the eight saved by water
God's patience and the few preserved

schools.summary: The parallel drawn: 'the like figure (antitypon — antitype, corresponding figure) whereunto even baptism doth also now save us' — as Noah was saved through water, so baptism, the corresponding figure, 'saves' believers (the water of the flood prefiguring the water of baptism); but with the careful qualification 'not the putting away of the filth of the flesh (not the mere outward washing of the body, the external rite), but the answer (eperotema — the response, inquiry, pledge, or covenant-engagement) of a good conscience toward God' — it is not the outward water that saves but what baptism signifies and seals (the inward reality, the pledge of a good conscience, the soul's turning to God, the outward rite without inward grace saving no one); 'by the resurrection of Jesus Christ' — the saving efficacy grounded not in the water but in Christ's resurrection, baptism uniting the believer to the risen Christ. The voices uniformly guard against attributing salvation to the mere outward rite or to baptismal regeneration apart from faith (Barnes, Robertson, Henry, Calvin).

Cyril: baptism saves by the resurrection of Jesus Christ — not the mere washing of the body but the answer of a good conscience, the believer united to the risen Christ; the saving power is in what baptism signifies and in Christ's resurrection, not in the outward element.

themes: even baptism doth also now save us (not the putting away of the filth of the flesh, but the answer of a good conscience toward God)
by the resurrection of Jesus Christ
baptism saves not by the outward washing but by the answer of a good conscience
the antitype of Noah's deliverance, grounded in the resurrection

schools.summary: The completion of the account of Christ: 'who is gone into heaven (ascended), and is on the right hand of God (the place of supreme honour and authority); angels and authorities and powers being made subject (subordinated) unto him' — Christ, after his suffering and resurrection, ascended and enthroned at God's right hand (the exaltation that crowns his humiliation, the position of highest dignity and power where he reigns in glory, his redeeming work finished); all orders of created beings, including the highest angelic ranks (authorities and powers), placed under his dominion, the whole heavenly hierarchy submitting to the exalted Christ, his sovereignty universal (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the exalted Christ, seated at God's right hand, has all things made subject to him — angels, authorities, and powers; the whole created order, even the highest heavenly ranks, bows to his universal dominion, the suffering Saviour now the enthroned Lord of all.

themes: Who is gone into heaven, and is on the right hand of God
angels and authorities and powers being made subject unto him
Christ exalted to God's right hand, all powers subject to him
the universal sovereignty of the exalted Christ
